coredata.dc:title: Ultrasonic modulation of phase separation and corrosion resistance for ternary Cu-Sn-Bi immiscible alloy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, J.M.
Wu, W.H.
Zhai, W.
Wei, B.
coredata.dc:description: The effect of power ultrasound on the liquid phase separation of ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy is experimentally investigated, which shows that as compared with the layered structure formed under static condition, the macrosegregation resulted from liquid phase separation is remarkably reduced with the increase of ultrasonic amplitude. A homogenous microstructure characterized by refined (Bi) particles dispersing uniformly on the (Cu3Sn) matrix is obtained when the ultrasonic amplitude reaches the highest value of 24 μm. This is mainly ascribed to the ultrasonically induced cavitation and acoustic streaming, which promotes the nucleation, the fragmentation, and the dispersion of (Bi) droplets. The finally solidified immiscible alloy exhibits obvious improvements in electrochemical corrosion resistance, microhardness and wear-resisting if compared with those in static solidification. These results prove that applying power ultrasound is an effective way to modulate the liquid phase separation and enhance the applied performance for immiscible alloys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Power ultrasound
Liquid phase separation
Electrochemical corrosion resistance
Mechanical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultrasonic modulation of liquid phase separation
Dynamic mechanism of liquid phase separation within ultrasonic field
Electrochemical corrosion resistance after ultrasonic treatment

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are very grateful to Q.Y. Peng, A.M. Ma, B.J. Wang and Y. Xiao for their technical assistance and helpful discussion. This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51727803 , 51327901 and 51571164 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermal analysis and near-equilibrium solidification characteristics of ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy: (a) alloy location in Cu-Sn-Bi phase diagram [21], (b) DTA curves, (c) solidification morphology of DTA alloy sample (The inserts are the enlarged view of Cu3Sn-rich and Bi-rich zones respectively), (d) XRD patterns of alloy samples solidified with various ultrasonic amplitudes.
Microstructural profiles of ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy solidified under different ultrasonic conditions: (a) A 0 = 0 μm, (b) A 0 = 14.5 μm, (c) A 0 = 20.3 μm, (d) A 0 = 24 μm.

Phase separation characteristics of ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy: (a) uniformity degree versus ultrasonic amplitudes, (b-c) solute distribution of Cu, Sn and Bi elements under the ultrasonic amplitude of 0 and 24 μm, (d) size distribution of secondary (Bi) particles versus ultrasonic amplitude.

The calculated Stokes motion and Marangoni migration of (Bi) particles, and flow velocity in ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy melt: (a) Stokes and Marangoni motion velocities versus droplet radius, (b) the maximum and average acoustic streaming velocities.

Phase separation mechanisms of ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy without and with ultrasound: (a) homogeneous alloy melt, (b) static, (c) with ultrasound. Here, V s, V m and V a denote the Stokes motion, Marangoni migration and the acoustic streaming, respectively.

Electrochemical corrosion resistance of Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy in 3.5% NaCl solution: (a) polarization curves, (b) corrosion current density and area fraction of (Bi) phase, (c-d) corrosion morphologies of alloy samples solidified with ultrasonic amplitudes of 0 and 24 μm.

Mechanical properties of Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy after ultrasonic treatment: (a) the average microhardness along the vertical direction, (b) average friction coefficient and abrasion loss versus ultrasonic amplitude.
Physical parameters used in calculations for Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy.

row.entry: Sound velocity of liquid alloy c, m·s−1
Density of alloy melt ρ, Kg·m−3
Density of L1(Cu3Sn) phase ρ 1, Kg·m−3
Density of L2(Bi) phase ρ 2, Kg·m−3
Viscosity of L1(Cu3Sn) phase μ1, Pa·s
Viscosity of L2(Bi) phase μ2, Pa·s
Viscosity of alloy melt μ, Pa·s
Acoustic attenuation coefficient of alloy melt α, Np·m−1
Heat conductivity of homogeneous melt λ, W·m−1·K−1
Heat conductivity of L1 (Cu3Sn) phase λ 1, W·m−1·K−1
Heat conductivity of L2 (Bi) phase λ 2, W·m−1·K−1
Heat capacity of alloy melt C p, J·Kg−1·K−1

head.ce:title: Ultrasonic modulation of phase separation and corrosion resistance for ternary Cu-Sn-Bi immiscible alloy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710072, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: P.O. Box 624, 127, Youyi West Road, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710072, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effect of power ultrasound on the liquid phase separation of ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy is experimentally investigated, which shows that as compared with the layered structure formed under static condition, the macrosegregation resulted from liquid phase separation is remarkably reduced with the increase of ultrasonic amplitude. A homogenous microstructure characterized by refined (Bi) particles dispersing uniformly on the (Cu3Sn) matrix is obtained when the ultrasonic amplitude reaches the highest value of 24 μm. This is mainly ascribed to the ultrasonically induced cavitation and acoustic streaming, which promotes the nucleation, the fragmentation, and the dispersion of (Bi) droplets. The finally solidified immiscible alloy exhibits obvious improvements in electrochemical corrosion resistance, microhardness and wear-resisting if compared with those in static solidification. These results prove that applying power ultrasound is an effective way to modulate the liquid phase separation and enhance the applied performance for immiscible alloys.

ce:section.ce:para: Immiscible alloys, such as Al-Pb and Zn-Bi, show low friction coefficient and excellent wear resistance if the soft Pb or Bi phase is uniformly dispersed in a hard matrix like Al or Cu [1–4]. They could be potential bearing materials and have a rich variety of applications as functional and structural materials in the industry. Unfortunately, this kind of alloy usually undergoes liquid-liquid phase separation, and the large density difference between the two immiscible liquids results in the formation of heavily segregated or even layered structure [5,6], which leads to their poor applied performances. Therefore, how to fabricate immiscible alloy with homogenous microstructure has aroused great research interest in the field of material science and technology. In recent years, numerous efforts have been made on using various external fields, such as electric field [1], centrifugal force field [4], microgravity field [6] and magnetic field [7], to investigate the kinetics of liquid phase separation and suppression of macrosegregation during the casting process of immiscible alloys. However, it still remains a great challenge to produce uniform structure for bulk immiscible alloys.

Ultrasonic processing, which brings about nonlinear effects including cavitation and acoustic streaming into the solidifying alloy, has been found to be an effective way to modulate the solidification process to gain refined structure with superior mechanical properties [8–16]. Most of the previous work devotes much attention to the dendritically solidified Al or Mg based alloys. It is shown that power ultrasound not only promotes the grain refinement but also enhances the homogeneity of the microstructure, thereby strengthening the alloy castings [9–13,16]. Nevertheless, the effect of power ultrasound on liquid phase separation and monotectic solidification of immiscible alloys lacks systematically investigation, though some previous work showed that the mechanical properties of immiscible alloys are improved by power ultrasound [14,15].

Ternary Cu-Sn-Bi alloy system has received much attention due to its extensive application in transient-liquid-phase (TLP) processing and Pb-free solder in electronic packaging technology [17–20]. By thermodynamic calculation, the monotectic composition and miscibility gap region of (Sn0.4Cu0.6)100-
x Bi x (wt%) alloys were obtained by Ma et al. [19]. Kodai Doi et al. also found that a two-phase separation zone exists in the ternary Cu-Sn-Bi system [20].

In present work, ultrasonic field is introduced into the liquid phase separation and solidification process of ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy. The liquid phase separation mechanism within ultrasonic field is discussed based on the experimental results and theoretical analysis. In addition, the electrochemical corrosion resistance and mechanical properties of this alloy after ultrasonic processing are also investigated.

The Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy was prepared by using ingots of pure Cu (99.9 wt%), Bi (99.99 wt%), and Sn (99.9 wt%). The ultrasonic solidification experiments were performed with a solidification apparatus incorporated with an ultrasonic generator at a frequency of 18.5 kHz. A sine function was used to describe the displacement of the ultrasonic horn, i.e. A = A 0sin(2πft + φ), where A was the displacement at time t, A 0 was the amplitude, f and φ were the frequency and phase difference, respectively. During experiment, pure Cu, Bi, and Sn elements with a weight of about 60 g were contained in a Φ 22 × 25 mm graphite crucible and melted in an electric resistance furnace. The alloy melt was heated and held at about 1273 K for 20 min to form a homogeneous liquid. Then, different ultrasonic vibration amplitudes (A 0 = 0, 10.5, 14.5, 20.3, and 24 μm), which measured with a Model JY-J200 ultrasonic amplitude measuring instrument manufactured by Suzhou Sonic Ultrasonic Technology Co., Ltd., were applied into the alloy melt from the top until it completely solidified.

After experiments, the solidified alloy samples were cut longitudinally along the central axis, and then mounted, polished and etched. The phase constitution and microstructural characteristics were analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an INCA Energy 300 energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). Image analysis software Image-Pro Plus 6.0 was used for the quantitative metallographic analysis to determine the characteristic length of solidification microstructure. In addition, the differential thermal analysis (DTA) experiment is performed with a WCR-2D differential thermal analyzer at a scan rate of 10 K·min−1 to explore the phase transitions and solidification microstructure of Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy under near-equilibrium condition.

The electrochemical corrosion resistance was tested by a CHI660E electrochemical workstation. The three-electrode system was used for electrochemical experiments, which consisted of the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, a Pt wire as an auxiliary electrode and a specimen (with a specific exposed area) as the working electrode. Linear polarization curves were measured by potentiostat and neutral 3.5% sodium chloride aqueous solution was adopted as an electrolyte for electrochemical study at room temperature. The anodic polarigation was carried out at a sweep rate of 10 mV s−1 and the scan potential range was from −2 to +2 V. Tafel fitting method was utilized to analyze the polarization curves and obtain the corrosion current density (I).

The Vickers microhardness (H v) of solidified alloy samples was measured at ambient condition by a digital microhardness tester (HXD-1000TMC/LCD) under an applied load of 50 g with a dwell time of 20 s. Furthermore, the wear-resisting property were determined by an HT-1000 high-temperature friction/wear tester with a load of 500 g and a rotational speed of 224 rpm at room temperature. The material of the corresponding wear ring was GCr15 (quenching, HRC58-60) and the wear time was 30 min.

The equilibrium phase diagram of the Cu-Sn-Bi ternary system is shown in Fig. 1 (a) [21], in which the location of Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi is marked as a red circle. According to the DTA curves presented in Fig. 1(b), six endothermic and six exothermic peaks are observed during the heating and cooling periods, respectively. Combined with the DTA results and the published Cu-Sn-Bi phase diagram [21–23], it can be determined that the liquid miscibility gap of the two immiscible liquids is in the temperature range from 1015 to 1118 K on heating. During cooling, the first small exothermic deviation from the baseline at 1097 K is due to the separation of liquid Cu3Sn-rich and Bi-rich phases L → L1(Cu3Sn-rich) + L2(Bi-rich). The two following sharp exothermic peaks denote the monotectic reaction L1 → γCu3Sn + L2 and the solid phase transition γ → εCu3Sn. After this, the eutectic transformation L2→(Sn) + ηCu6Sn5 and peritectic reaction L2 + εCu3Sn→(Bi) + ηCu6Sn5 take place at 487 and 435 K, respectively [22]. The last weak peak corresponds to the eutectic reaction L2 → (Sn) + (Bi). The whole view of the DTA alloy sample is given in Fig. 1(c), which is characterized by an evident core-shell structure. The formation of core-shell structure is ascribed to the surface segregation of (Bi) caused by concentration differences due to the fact that the surface tension of Bi-rich liquid is lower than that of Cu3Sn-rich liquid [19,24]. The EDS analysis shows that the black phase and the white phase in the inner core are the Cu3Sn and (Bi) phases, respectively. By contrast, the composition of the dark phase in the outer shell is Sn-74.47 at% Cu, referring to Cu3Sn phase. The grey phase is Cu6Sn5, and the composition of the eutectic region is Cu-10.37 at% Sn-63.64 at% Bi, which should be composed of (Sn), (Bi) and η(Cu6Sn5) phases.

XRD patterns for Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy samples are presented in Fig. 1(d). It can be demonstrated that the Cu3Sn and (Bi) are the two main phases in all the alloy samples regardless of ultrasonic amplitude. Besides, there also exists a small amount of minor Cu6Sn5 intermetallic compound and (Sn) solid solution phase. These are consistent with the phase constitution of the DTA alloy samples, and suggest that the ultrasonic field does not change the phase composition of solidified ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasonic modulation of liquid phase separation
ce:section.ce:para: The macroscopic structural patterns of ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy samples processed by different ultrasonic conditions are shown in Fig. 2 . As given in Fig. 2(a), during the static condition, the alloy sample exhibits serious macrosegregation, in which a dark Cu3Sn-rich zone occupying about 85% volume fraction at the top and a bright Bi-rich zone at the remaining bottom part. In the upper zone, many tiny Bi-rich particles, which are spherical or short clavate, distribute on (Cu3Sn) matrix (Fig. 2(a2)). In the bottom zone, by contrast, the microstructure is featured by (Bi + Sn) eutectic and Cu3Sn dendrites dispersing on the (Bi) matrix (Fig. 2(a3)). The macrosegregation pattern alters significantly when the power ultrasound is introduced. As seen in Fig. 2(b), when the ultrasonic amplitude rises up to 14.5 μm, the segregation layer of Bi-rich phase at the bottom disappears, while a large number of spherical Bi-rich particles aggregate on Cu3Sn-rich matrix at the sample bottom, and there also more Bi-rich particles are found in the upper part as compared with those under static condition. If the ultrasonic amplitude is further increased, the distribution uniformity of Bi-rich particles is improved. Fig. 2(c) depicts the microstructures solidified under the ultrasonic amplitude of 20.3 μm, in which the Bi-rich particles are significantly refined and their distribution height increases appreciably. In particular, once the ultrasonic amplitude reaches 24 μm, the Bi-rich particles, which become the irregular shape, almost evenly distribute in the whole alloy sample to form a uniform structure, as shown in Fig. 2(d).

The aggregation height of Bi-rich zone versus ultrasonic amplitude is illustrated in Fig. 3 (a). Here, α h = H s/H 0 is defined as the distribution uniformity degree of the secondary (Bi) phase particles, where H S denotes the height of (Bi)-rich zone, and H 0 stands for the total height of the alloy sample. Evidently, the uniformity degree increases from 18% to nearly 100% with the ultrasonic amplitude varies from 0 to 24 μm. Meanwhile, to explore the macroscopic solute distribution, the contents for Cu, Sn and Bi elements are quantitatively determined by the EDS analysis within the five equal zones from sample top to bottom. For the alloy sample solidified statically, as shown in Fig. 3(b), the Bi content increases drastically whereas those of Sn and Cu elements decrease significantly. The solute content in each zone of the alloy sample seriously deviates from its original composition of 20%Bi, 48%Cu and 32%Sn. The segregation of the elements within the ultrasonicated alloy is suppressed considerably and the difference of elements content from top to bottom becomes weak as ultrasonic amplitude elevates. Once the ultrasonic amplitude attains the highest value of 24 μm, the solute distribution in all the zones of the alloy sample keeps almost the same (Fig. 3(c)), indicating the formation of homogeneous structure from a view of solute distribution. Furthermore, the size distribution of (Bi) particles in different ultrasonicated alloy samples is also investigated and the results are given in Fig. 3 (d). As ultrasonic amplitudes increases, the average grain size (D ave) decreases from 282 to 155 μm and the mode of grain size (D pmax) drops from about 250 to 125 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic mechanism of liquid phase separation within ultrasonic field
ce:section.ce:para: From the microstructural evolution and solute distribution characteristics of Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy, it can be seen that the application of ultrasonic field into the alloy melt not only inhibits the macrosegregation caused by liquid phase separation but also refines the secondary (Bi) phase.

In the conventional solidification, the formation of severe segregated structure can be briefly elucidated as follows. Firstly, the secondary (Bi) phase nucleates as clusters in the homogeneous alloy melt. Then, the (Bi) droplets experience diffusion growth, including the collisions, the coagulation, and the Ostwald ripening. Finally, those (Bi) droplets aggregate and move within the liquid alloy driven by the surface tension force and gravitational force. The sinking of the secondary (Bi) droplets under gravitational force can be described as Stokes motion (V s) [25]
(1)
V s
=
2 g Δ ρ
μ 1
+
μ 2
3
μ 1
2
μ 1
+ 3
μ 2
r 2
,
where Δρ is the density difference between the liquid Bi-rich and Cu3Sn-rich phases, g is the gravitational acceleration, μ1 and μ2 are the viscosities of the liquid Cu3Sn-rich phase and Bi-rich phase, respectively, and r is the radius of Bi-rich droplet. Besides, the Marangoni migration (Vm ) induced by the temperature gradient from sample edge to center also affects the motion of liquid (Bi) droplets, which can be expressed as
(2)
V m
=
2
∂ σ
/
∂ T
∂ T
/
∂ x
λ 1
2
μ 1
+ 3
μ 2
2
λ 1
+ 3
λ 2
r ,
where λ 1 and λ 2 are the heat conductivities of L1(Cu3Sn) and secondary L2(Bi) droplets, respectively. ∂T/∂x is the transverse temperature gradient and its measured value is 1 K·mm−1, and ∂σ/∂T is the interfacial energy gradient. The Stokes motion and Marangoni migration velocities are calculated by Eqs. (1) and (2), and the values of major parameters used in the equations are listed in Table 1 . As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the Stokes motion velocity increases from 0 to 24.68 mm·s−1, while the Marangoni migration velocity changes from 0 to 2.22 mm·s−1 when the radius of Bi-rich droplets varies from 0 to 100 μm. This numerical result shows that the Marangoni migration velocity is much lower than the Stokes motion velocity of Bi-rich droplets with radius greater than 50 μm, indicating that the effect of Stokes motion dominates the aggregation and sedimentation of the Bi-rich phase droplets during the liquid phase separation. Consequently, the layered structure with a clear boundary is formed under static condition. The above mentioned liquid phase separation process under conventional condition is schematically illustrated in Fig. 5 (a) and (b).

When power ultrasound is introduced, the nonlinear effects during the propagation of power ultrasound in the alloy melt, such as the cavitation and acoustic streaming, are considered to be the main factors affecting the liquid phase separation. The ultrasonic cavitation, which results from the formation, growth and collapse of microbubbles, can produce large quantities of instantaneous ultra-strong shockwaves and high-speed microjets in the local regions of the alloy melt [27]. Besides, the attenuation of sound wave induced by liquid viscosity in the alloy melt is able to produce large-scale convection, which is known as acoustic streaming [28]. The acoustic pressure field can be calculated by solving the time wave harmonic wave equation [29,30]
(3)
∇ ·
-
1
ρ c
∇ p
-
ω 2
ρ c
c c 2
p = 0

Here, p is the sound pressure, and ω is the angular frequency defined as ω = 2πf (f is the frequency). ρc and cc respectively denote the complex density and sound speed, which are expressed as
(4)
ρ c
=
ρ
c 2
c c 2
,
c c
=
ω k
in which ρ means the media density, and c is the sound speed in media. The wave number k is written as follow:
(5)
k =
ω c
- i α
where α is the acoustic attenuation coefficient.

The velocity of acoustic streaming can be obtained by the momentum transport equation and the continuity equation [29]:
(6)
- ∇ ·
( - p
I →
- τ )
+
ρ 0
(
V →
a
· ∇ )
V →
a
=
F →
,
(7)
∇ ·
V →
a
= 0 ,
where p is the sound pressure in liquid, I is the unit matrix, τ and Va are the viscous stress tensor and liquid velocity, respectively. F is the force per unit volume that causes acoustic flow, which can be simply written as:
(8)
F =
2 α
ρ 0
c 0 2
p
2
.

The acoustic pressure distributions are firstly calculated by Eq. (3), and then the volume force F is computed by substituting the value of the sound pressure into Eq. (8). Finally, the liquid velocity distribution is obtained by Eqs. (6) and (7). The related physical parameters used in simulation by finite element method are also given in Table 1, and the power coefficient of ultrasound from the air to the alloy melt is set as 0.7 empirically in all the simulations [31]. The maximum (V a-max) and average (V a-ave) liquid velocities in the alloy melt within different ultrasonic amplitudes are extracted and presented in Fig. 4(b). The two velocities both show a positive correlation with the ultrasonic amplitude and respectively attain 35 and 14.6 mm·s−1 under the highest amplitude of 24 μm.

When power ultrasound is introduced into the alloy melt, the cavitation effect increases the local undercooling, which promotes the heterogeneous nucleation of secondary liquid (Bi) phase (Fig. 5(c1)). Meanwhile, the resulting strong shockwaves break up those secondary (Bi) droplets into small ones in their diffusion and growth stages (Fig. 5(c2)). Moreover, due to the strong shearing forces induced by the circular flow, the (Bi) droplets would encounter great resistance when they tend to coagulate with each other and therefore hard to sink down the bottom, as shown in Fig. 5(c3). Consequently, if the ultrasonic amplitude is low, the sinking of the (Bi) phase droplets can only be partially reduced or suppressed, which leads to the formation of segregated structure. Increasing the ultrasonic amplitude could strength the intensity of both cavitation and acoustic streaming, thus resulting in the more and more uniformly distribution of refined (Bi) phase particles in the (Cu3Sn) matrix, as presented in Fig. 5(c4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical corrosion resistance after ultrasonic treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The potentiodynamic polarization curves obtained in 3.5% NaCl solution at room temperature for ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy is plotted in Fig. 6 (a), in which the corrosion potential is negative, suggesting the high corrosion sensitivity of this alloy. Fig. 6(b) shows that the corrosion current density exhibits a downward trend with the increase of ultrasonic amplitude, which corresponds to the lower dissolution rate or higher corrosion resistance [32,33]. This trend means the electrochemical corrosion resistance improves with the application of ultrasonic field. The corrosion morphologies of alloy samples solidified under static condition and with ultrasonic amplitude of 24 μm after electrochemical corrosion are shown in Fig. 6(c) and (d). It can be seen that for both of the two alloy samples, severe corrosion with visible cracks occurs in the (Bi) phase while the Cu3Sn phase keeps intact by the formation of a corrosion product layer on its surface. These indicate that the corrosion resistance of Cu3Sn intermetallic compound is stronger than that of Bi-rich phase. This can be further demonstrated by the fact that the corrosion resistance enhances with the decrease of volume fraction for secondary (Bi) phase when ultrasonic amplitude rises (Fig. 6(b)). In addition, the dispersive distribution of (Bi) phase on Cu3Sn matrix increases the structural uniformity and the number density of grain boundaries, which may enhance the charge transfer resistance and polarization resistance, thereby leading to superior corrosion resistance performance of Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy [34–36].

The average microhardness values of ten zones along the vertical direction of Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy samples are presented in Fig. 7 (a). Evidently, the microhardness exhibits great differences under static condition, whose value is more than 300 HV in the Cu3Sn-rich zone whereas it sharply reduces to about 20 HV in the Bi-rich zone at the sample bottom. With introducing the power ultrasound into the alloy melt, the difference of average microhardness from the top to the bottom decreases. Especially, when ultrasonic amplitude reaches the maximum of 24 μm, the values of average microhardness from the sample top to the bottom are all around 300 HV, indicating the average microhardness along the vertical direction of alloy sample tends to be uniform.

The average friction coefficient α f of different ultrasonicated Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy samples at room temperature is displayed in Fig. 7(b). The average friction coefficient shows the highest value of 0.53 under static condition, and it reduces linearly with the increase of ultrasonic amplitude. The abrasion loss of the alloy sample Δm is always applied in judging the standard of wear-resisting properties [37]. As presented in Fig. 7(b), the abrasion loss decreases monotonically with the increase of the ultrasonic amplitude. The minimum value is 63% lower than that of statically solidified sample. Therefore, the uniform distribution and the refinement of secondary (Bi) particles lead to a remarkable enhancement in wear resistance of ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy.

The liquid phase separation mechanism of ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy within ultrasonic field and its resultant electrochemical corrosion resistance and mechanical properties are investigated systematically. The main results are summarized as follows:
(1)
The liquid phase separation of L → L1(Cu3Sn-rich) + L2(Bi-rich) takes place prior to solidification in ternary Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi immiscible alloy and the critical temperature for this liquid phase separation is determined to be 1097 K. Meanwhile, the nucleation temperature of primary Cu3Sn solid phase and solidus temperature are determined as 1015 and 413 K, respectively. The final solidification microstructure is composed of two main phases of Cu3Sn and (Bi) as well as a small amount of minor (Sn) and Cu6Sn5 phases.
(2)
The introduction of ultrasonic field reduces the macrosegregation of the two immiscible liquids, and promotes the formation of refined (Bi) phase uniformly dispersed on Cu3Sn matrix. This is mainly attributed to cavitation effect and acoustic streaming, which not only reduces the coalescence probability but also inhibits the Stokes sedimentation of Bi-rich droplets during liquid phase separation.
(3)
The electrochemical corrosion resistance is improved as ultrasonic amplitude rises. This may be ascribed to the increased area fraction of (Cu3Sn) phase and the enhancement of polarization resistance caused by the refinement and homogenization of the solidification microstructure.
(4)
With the increase of ultrasonic amplitude, the microhardness along the vertical direction of Cu-32%Sn-20%Bi alloy is improved, and the wear resistance is enhanced by a factor of 1.63.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to Q.Y. Peng, A.M. Ma, B.J. Wang and Y. Xiao for their technical assistance and helpful discussion. This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51727803, 51327901 and 51571164).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2019.01.029.
